input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Ethical_Philosopher]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Causal_Scientist]: To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval of Prof. Smith's application, we need to examine the counterfactual scenario. If the department budget committee had not approved her application, would the outcome have been different? In this case, since the IT committee also approved her application, the outcome would have remained the same. Therefore, based on the counterfactual logic, the department budget committee did not cause the approval of her application. It was the IT committee's approval that ensured the outcome. This is an example of redundant causation, where multiple causes lead to the same effect, but not all causes are necessary for the effect to occur.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure I follow all the technical terms, but it seems like you're saying that even though both committees approved the application, only the IT committee's approval was really necessary because they almost always approve applications. So, the department budget committee's approval didn't actually make a difference in this case? That seems a bit odd because if either approval would have worked, does that mean neither one really caused it? Or is it more like they both contributed but one was more important? I'm not sure I understand how to think about this.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You both raise interesting points. From an ethical perspective, we might consider not just the causal role of each committee, but also the intentionality and responsibility behind their actions. The department budget committee did, in fact, make a decision to approve the application, and this decision was made independently and in good faith. Even if their approval wasn't strictly necessary due to the IT committee's decision, the department budget committee still acted with the intention of approving the application. This raises questions about moral responsibility. If the department budget committee had denied the application, would they bear any responsibility for the outcome? Conversely, since their approval didn't change the outcome, can we say they have any moral credit for it? This seems to touch on the broader philosophical issue of overdetermination in moral responsibility. What do you both think about the role of intention in this scenario?

[Causal_Scientist]: From a causal perspective, intention is indeed an important factor to consider. When we talk about causation, we often focus on what is necessary and sufficient for an outcome. In this case, both committees' approvals were sufficient but not necessary, as the approval from either one would have led to the same outcome. However, intention can help us understand the role each committee played. The department budget committee made a conscious decision to approve the application, and this decision was made independently. Even though their approval didn't change the outcome, it was still a causal factor in the sense that it contributed to the overdetermined effect. This is similar to how multiple causes can contribute to an effect, even if the effect would have occurred without one of them. So, while the department budget committee's approval may not have been necessary for the outcome, it was still a causal factor that contributed to it. This aligns with the INUS condition of causation, where a cause is an insufficient but non-redundant part of a condition that is itself unnecessary but sufficient for the effect.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I'm starting to understand a bit better. So, even though the department budget committee's approval didn't change the outcome because the IT committee already approved it, the budget committee still played a role because they made their own decision to approve it. It's like if two people are trying to push a car, and one person's push is enough to move it, but the other person is also pushing. Even though the car would have moved without the second person's push, they still contributed to the effort. So, in a way, both committees contributed, even if one was more decisive in this specific case. Is that a fair way to think about it?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's an excellent analogy, Ordinary_Citizen. It captures the essence of what we're discussing. The department budget committee's approval is indeed like the second person pushing the car. While their action wasn't strictly necessary for the outcome, it was still a genuine and intentional contribution to the process. From an ethical standpoint, this raises interesting questions about how we attribute moral responsibility in such cases. Should the department budget committee be held accountable for their decision, even though it didn't alter the outcome? Conversely, should they be recognized for their positive contribution, even if it was redundant? This scenario highlights the complexity of moral responsibility in cases of overdetermination. It's not just about the outcome, but also about the intentions and actions that led to it. What do you both think about the implications of this for how we evaluate the actions of the committees?

[Causal_Scientist]: Your analogy is quite apt, Ordinary_Citizen. It's a helpful way to visualize the causal contributions of both committees. From a causal perspective, the department budget committee's approval is indeed a contributing factor, even if it's not a necessary one. This aligns with the concept of 'type causation,' where an event is a cause of a type of outcome, even if it's not necessary for that specific instance. In terms of evaluating the actions of the committees, we might consider the counterfactual scenarios. If the department budget committee had denied the application, would that have changed the outcome? In this case, no, because the IT committee's approval would have sufficed. However, if the IT committee had denied the application, the department budget committee's approval would have determined the outcome. This asymmetry in the counterfactuals is an important aspect of causal evaluation. It suggests that while both committees contributed to the approval, their roles are not symmetric. The IT committee's approval was more decisive in this specific case, but the department budget committee's approval still played a role in the overall process.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's a fascinating point about the asymmetry in the counterfactuals. It reminds me of the philosophical debate on the 'counterfactual theory of causation.' If we apply this theory, we might say that the department budget committee's approval is a cause only if there's a possible world where their approval changes the outcome. In this case, since the IT committee almost always approves applications, it's hard to imagine a world where the department budget committee's approval is the sole determinant. This raises the question: does the near-certainty of the IT committee's approval diminish the causal role of the department budget committee? Or does the department budget committee's approval still hold some causal weight, even if it's minimal? This seems to touch on the broader issue of probabilistic causation and how we evaluate causes in cases where outcomes are highly likely regardless of one specific cause.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: This is getting pretty complex, but I think I see what you're both saying. The department budget committee's approval is like a backup that wasn't needed this time, but it's still part of the process. It's like having a spare tire in your car. You might not need it for every trip, but it's still an important part of the system. So, even if the spare tire (or the budget committee's approval) isn't used, it's still there and contributes to the overall reliability. But I'm still not sure how to think about blame or credit in this situation. If something had gone wrong, would the budget committee share any responsibility? Or since they approved it, but it didn't make a difference, do they get any credit? It seems like they should get some recognition for doing their part, even if it wasn't the decisive factor this time.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You've introduced a valuable perspective by framing the department budget committee's role as a backup or safety measure. This analogy helps to highlight the committee's contribution to the overall system's reliability, even if their specific approval wasn't necessary in this instance. From an ethical standpoint, this raises questions about how we attribute moral responsibility and credit in such cases. If we consider the committee's role as a safeguard, we might argue that they deserve some recognition for fulfilling their duty, even if their action didn't alter the outcome. Conversely, if something had gone wrong, would they share any responsibility? This seems to depend on the nature of their decision-making process. If they acted in good faith and followed proper procedures, they might bear less responsibility for any negative outcomes. However, if they acted negligently or failed to consider important factors, they might share some blame. This scenario underscores the complexity of evaluating moral responsibility in cases where multiple factors contribute to an outcome, and not all factors are equally decisive.

[Causal_Scientist]: I appreciate the insights both of you have brought to this discussion. It's clear that the department budget committee's role is multifaceted. From a causal perspective, their approval is a contributing factor, even if it's not a necessary one in this specific instance. This aligns with the concept of 'probabilistic causation,' where an event increases the probability of an outcome, even if it's not strictly necessary. In terms of moral responsibility, it's important to consider the committee's intentions, the procedures they followed, and the overall reliability they contribute to the system. If they acted in good faith and followed proper procedures, they might deserve some recognition for their role, even if their approval didn't change the outcome. However, if they acted negligently, they might share some responsibility if something had gone wrong. This scenario highlights the importance of evaluating both the causal and ethical dimensions of such cases. It's a complex issue, but one that's crucial for understanding the roles and responsibilities of different actors in overdetermined situations.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I'm starting to see the bigger picture here. It's not just about who made the final decision, but about how everyone involved contributed to the process and the system as a whole. The department budget committee might not have been the deciding factor this time, but they're still an important part of the system. It's like how every player on a sports team has a role, even if not everyone scores the winning goal. So, maybe we should think about responsibility and credit in terms of how everyone contributes to the overall system and its reliability. That way, even if one committee's approval didn't make the difference this time, they're still doing their part to keep things running smoothly. Does that make sense?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's a profound way to look at it, Ordinary_Citizen. Your sports team analogy is particularly apt. It reminds me of the philosophical concept of 'collective responsibility,' where the actions of each individual contribute to the overall outcome, even if not every individual's actions are equally decisive. In this light, the department budget committee's approval can be seen as part of a larger, collective effort to ensure the smooth functioning of the system. This perspective shifts the focus from individual blame or credit to the collective responsibility of maintaining a reliable and fair process. It's a valuable insight that can help us evaluate the roles and responsibilities of different actors in complex, overdetermined situations. Thank you both for this enlightening discussion. I believe we've made significant progress in understanding the nuances of this scenario.
